Paragraph.Text: the amount, by class of contributor, of contributions for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes that were received during the period referred to in paragraph (1)(b);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: subject to paragraph (c), for each contributor who made contributions of a total amount of more than $200 for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes during the period referred to in paragraph (1)(b), their name, address and class, and the amount and date of each contribution;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a numbered company that is a contributor referred to in paragraph (b), the name of the chief executive officer or president of that company; and

Subsection.Text: Subsection 357.01(7) of the Act is repealed.
For the purposes of paragraphs (4)(a) and (b), the following are the classes of contributor:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: individuals;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: businesses;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: commercial organizations;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: governments;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: trade unions;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: corporations without share capital other than trade unions; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: unincorporated organizations or associations other than trade unions.

Section.Text: The portion of section 357.1 of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Prohibition â use of anonymous contributions
Section.Text: No third party shall use a contribution for the purpose of any of the following if the third party does not know the name and address of the contributor:

No third party shall use a contribution for the purpose of any of the following if the third party does not know the name and address of the contributor or is otherwise unable to determine within which class of contributor referred to in subsection 359(6) the contributor falls:
The Act is amended by adding the following after section 357.1:

Subsection.Text: A third party shall use only contributions from Canadian individuals to pay for regulated expenses and use, as regulated expenses, only property or services that are contributions from Canadian individuals.

However, if the total amount of contributions received, from all sources and for any purpose, by the third party during the previous year is equal to or less than 10% of its revenue for that year, the third party may use its own funds, namely funds that are not contributions made to it, to pay for regulated expenses and use, as regulated expenses, property or services that it has provided. In that case, the third party shall include financial statements for the previous year in the third-party expenses return referred to in section 359.

For the purposes of subsection (2), grants and contributions received from the Government of Canada or of a province or from a municipality are not included in determining the third partyâs revenue for the previous year.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Previous year â choice of third party
Subsection.Text: For the purposes of subsections (2) and (3), the third party may choose as their previous year either

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the calendar year that precedes the calendar year in which a pre-election period occurs, or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the fiscal year that precedes the fiscal year in which a pre-election period occurs.

Subsection.Text: This section does not apply in respect of a third party who is an individual or a third party that is not required to register under subsection 353(1).
The following definitions apply in this section.

Definition.Text: Canadian individualâmeans an individual who is a Canadian citizen or a permanent resident as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.â(particulier canadien)
regulated expensesâmeans any of the following expenses:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: partisan activity expenses in relation to partisan activities that are carried out during an election period;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: election advertising expenses in relation to election advertising messages that are transmitted during that period; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: election survey expenses in relation to election surveys that are conducted during that period.â(dÃ©penses rÃ©glementÃ©es)

Subsection.Text: Section 359 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (3):
If section 349.05 applies to the third party, the third-party expenses return shall include a list of the measures that the third party has taken in accordance with that section.
If subsection 349.95(2) or 358(2) applies with respect to the third party, the third-party expenses return shall include the financial statements referred to in that subsection.

If subsection 349.95(1) or 358(1) applies with respect to the third party, the third-party expenses return shall include the following for each Canadian individual whose contributions of a total amount of more than $200 were used to pay for the regulated expenses under that subsection:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: their name and address;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the amount of each contribution;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a list of the property or services that was contributed; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the date each contribution was made.

Subsection.Text: Paragraphs 359(4)(a) to (b.1) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the amount of contributions for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes that were received during the period beginning on the day after polling day at the general election previous to the polling day referred to in subsection (1) and ending on the polling day referred to in that subsection;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: for each contributor who made contributions of a total amount of more than $200 for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes during the period referred to in paragraph (a), their name and address and the amount and date of each contribution; and

Subsection.Text: The third-party expenses return shall include

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the amount, by class of contributor, of contributions for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes that were received during the period beginning the day after polling day at the general election previous to the polling day referred to in subsection (1) and ending on that polling day referred to in that subsection;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: subject to paragraph (b.1), for each contributor who made contributions of a total amount of more than $200 for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes during the period referred to in paragraph (a), their name, address and class, and the amount and date of each contribution;

Paragraph.Label: (b.1)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a numbered company that is a contributor referred to in paragraph (b), the name of the chief executive officer or president of that company; and

Subsection.Text: Subsection 359(6) of the Act is repealed.
For the purposes of paragraphs (4)(a) and (b), the following are the classes of contributor:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: individuals;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: businesses;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: commercial organizations;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: governments;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: trade unions;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: corporations without share capital other than trade unions; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: unincorporated organizations or associations other than trade unions.

Subsection.Text: Subsection 359(7) of the Act is replaced by the following:

If the third party is unable to identify which contributions were received during the period referred to in paragraph (4)(a) for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes, the third-party expenses return shall include the names and addresses of every contributor who contributed a total of more than $200 to it during that period.

If the third party is unable to identify which contributions were received during the period referred to in paragraph (4)(a) for partisan activity, partisan advertising, election advertising or election survey purposes, the third-party expenses return shall include, subject to paragraph (4)(b.1), the names and addresses of every contributor who contributed a total of more than $200 to it during that period.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 372:

Section.MarginalNote: Prohibition â cryptoasset, money order or payment product
Section.Text: No registered party, registered association, nomination contestant, candidate or leadership contestant shall accept a contribution that is in the form of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a cryptoasset, namely a digital asset protected by cryptographic measures;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a money order; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a prepaid payment product, namely a physical or electronic product that is or can be loaded with funds and that can be used to make withdrawals or purchase goods or services.

Section.MarginalNote: Return of cryptoasset, money order or payment product
Section.Text: If a person or entity referred to in section 372.1 receives a contribution referred to in that section, the chief agent of the registered party, the financial agent of the registered association, the official agent of the candidate or the financial agent of the nomination contestant or leadership contestant shall, within 30 days after becoming aware of the receipt of the contribution, take one of the following measures:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: return the contribution unused to the contributor;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: if it is not possible to return the contribution, destroy it unused;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: if it is not possible to return or destroy the contribution, convert it into money and pay the amount of it to the Chief Electoral Officer, who shall forward that amount to the Receiver General.

Section.Text: Section 384.2 of the Act and the heading before it are replaced by the following:

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 62:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the heading and section 384.2:

Subsection.Text: If a registered party organizes all or part of a regulated fundraising event for its own financial benefit or for the financial benefit of one of its registered associations, nomination contestants, candidates or leadership contestants, the registered party shall publish the information set out in subsection (2) in a prominent location on its Internet site for the period beginning five days before the day the regulated fundraising event takes place and ending at the time the event starts.
The information to be published under subsection (1) and provided under subsection (3) is the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the date, time and location of the regulated fundraising event;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the name of each entity or person referred to in paragraph 384.1(1)(a) for whose financial benefit the event is organized;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the name of each person referred to in subparagraph 384.1(1)(b)(i) who will be attending the event;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the total amount of contributions that a person will be required to have made, or the amount that they will be required to have paid, in order to attend the event; and

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the contact information of an individual who may be contacted and from whom further information about the event may be obtained.

Subsection.Text: If a regulated fundraising event is organized entirely by one or more persons or entities other than the registered party for whose financial benefit the event is organized, or for the financial benefit of whose registered association, nomination contestant, candidate or leadership contestant the event is organized, each person or entity that organizes the event or any part of it shall provide the information set out in subsection (2) to the registered party in time for the registered party to be able to publish the information as required under subsection (4).

If at least one person or entity referred to in subsection (3) complies with the obligation under that subsection â to provide information to the registered party in time for the registered party to be able to publish it â the registered party shall publish that information in a prominent location on its Internet site for the period beginning five days before the day the regulated fundraising event takes place and ending at the time the event starts.

If a registered party is required to publish information under subsection (1) or (4), it shall also notify the Chief Electoral Officer of the regulated fundraising event no later than five days before the day the event takes place.

If a registered party referred to in subsection (1) or (4) becomes aware, after publishing information under this section, of any change to that information, the registered party shall replace the old information on its Internet site with the new information as soon as feasible after becoming aware of the change.

If a person or entity referred to in subsection (3) becomes aware, after providing information to a registered party under this section, of any change to that information, the person or entity shall provide the new information to the registered party as soon as feasible after becoming aware of the change.

If new information is provided to a registered party under subsection (6), the registered party shall replace the old information on its Internet site with the new information as soon as feasible after being provided with it.
Subsections (1) to (7) do not apply in respect of a regulated fundraising event that takes place during the election period of a general election.

Section.Text: Subsection 384.3(13) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer shall publish reports provided under subsections (1), (6) and (8), and any corrected or revised versions of those reports, as soon as feasible after receiving them, in the manner that he or she considers appropriate. The reports that are published must limit the information on the location referred to in paragraph (2)(a) to the municipality, or its equivalent, and the province in which the event took place.

The Chief Electoral Officer shall publish reports provided under subsections (1), (6) and (8), and any corrected or revised versions of those reports, as soon as feasible after receiving them, in the manner that he or she considers appropriate.

Section.Text: Section 384.4 of the Act and the heading before it are repealed.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 64:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the heading and section 384.4:

Section.Text: If a registered party, a registered association, a nomination contestant, a candidate or a leadership contestant receives a contribution in respect of a regulated fundraising event in respect of which section 384.2 or 384.3 is not complied with, the chief agent of the registered party, the financial agent of the registered association, the official agent of the candidate or the financial agent of the nomination contestant or leadership contestant, as the case may be, shall, within 30 days after becoming aware of the non-compliance, return the contribution unused to the contributor or, if that is not possible, pay the amount of it â or, in the case of a non-monetary contribution, an amount of money equal to its commercial value â to the Chief Electoral Officer, who shall forward that amount to the Receiver General.
The Act is amended by adding the following after the heading of Division 2 of Part 18:
In this Division, personal information means information about an identifiable individual.
Paragraph 385(2)(k) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information; and

Subsection.Text: The application for registration shall include

Paragraph.Text: Â

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information, including

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: a statement indicating the types of personal information that the party collects and how it collects that information,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a statement indicating how the party protects personal information under its control,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: a statement indicating how the party uses personal information under its control and under what circumstances that personal information may be sold to any person or entity,

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: a statement indicating the training concerning the collection and use of personal information to be given to any employee of the party who could have access to personal information under the partyâs control,

Subparagraph.Label: (v)
Subparagraph.Text: a statement indicating the partyâs practices concerning

Clause.Label: (A)
Clause.Text: the collection and use of personal information created from online activity, and

Subparagraph.Label: (vi)
Subparagraph.Text: the name and contact information of a person to whom concerns regarding the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information can be addressed; and

Section.Text: Sections 385.1 and 385.2 of the Act are repealed.

Subsection.Text: Within three months after the day on which this section comes into force, the leader of a political party shall provide the Chief Electoral Officer with the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information referred to in paragraph 385(2)(k) and the Internet address referred to in paragraph 385(2)(l), if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: before the day on which this section comes into force, the leader of the party has applied under section 385 for the party to become a registered party but, as of that day, the Chief Electoral Officer has not yet informed the leader under subsection 389(1) whether or not the party is eligible for registration under section 387; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which this section comes into force, the party is

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: an eligible party, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a registered party.

Subsection.Text: If the leader of the political party does not comply with subsection (1), then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in paragraph (1)(a), the party is not eligible for registration under section 387;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in subparagraph (1)(b)(i), the party may not become a registered party under section 390; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in subparagraph (1)(b)(ii), the Chief Electoral Officer shall implement the procedure for non-voluntary deregistration set out in sections 415, 416 and 418.

Subsection.Text: If the leader of a political party provides the Chief Electoral Officer with the policy and the address referred to in subsection (1) in compliance with that subsection, or in compliance with section 415, then the policy and the address are deemed, as of the day on which they are provided, to be included in the application for registration referred to in subsection 385(2) in respect of the party.
Despite the definition personal information in subsection 2(1), for the purposes of this section, personal information means information about an identifiable individual.

In order to participate in public affairs by endorsing one or more of its members as candidates and supporting their election, any registered party or eligible party, as well as any person or organization acting on the partyâs behalf, including the partyâs candidates, electoral district associations, officers, agents, employees, volunteers and representatives, may, subject to this Act and any other applicable federal Act, collect, use, disclose, retain and dispose of personal information in accordance with the partyâs privacy policy.

The purpose of this section is to provide for a national, uniform, exclusive and complete regime applicable to registered parties and eligible parties respecting their collection, use, disclosure, retention and disposal of personal information.

Section.Text: Section 387 of the Act is amended by striking out âandâ at the end of paragraph (b), by adding âandâ at the end of paragraph (c) and by adding the following after paragraph (c):

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the Chief Electoral Officer is satisfied that the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information meets the requirements set out in subsection 444.4(1).

Section.Text: A political party whose leader has made an application under subsection 385(1) becomes eligible for registration if
Subsection 407(1) of the Act is amended by striking out âandâ at the end of paragraph (a), by adding âandâ at the end of paragraph (b) and by adding the following after paragraph (b):

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a statement certified by its privacy officer that the party complies with its policy for the protection of personal information.

Subsection.Text: On or before June 30 of every year, a registered party and an eligible party shall provide the Chief Electoral Officer with

Section.Text: Section 432 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: If section 372.2 applies to the registered party, the financial transactions return shall include a list of the measures that the chief agent of the registered party has taken in accordance with that section.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 444:

Heading.Label: SUBDIVISION C
Heading.TitleText: Personal Information Collected by Political Parties

Section.Text: The purpose of this subdivision is to provide for a national, uniform, exclusive and complete regime applicable to registered parties and eligible parties respecting their collection, use, disclosure, retention and disposal of personal information.

Section.MarginalNote: Collection, use, disclosure, retention and disposal
Section.Text: In order to participate in public affairs by endorsing one or more of its members as candidates and supporting their election, any registered party or eligible party, as well as any person or entity acting on the partyâs behalf, including the partyâs candidates, electoral district associations, officers, agents, employees, volunteers and representatives, may, subject to this Act and any other applicable federal Act, collect, use, disclose, retain and dispose of personal information in accordance with the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information.

Section.MarginalNote: Policy for the protection of personal information

Subsection.Text: A registered party or eligible party, as well as any person or entity acting on the partyâs behalf, including the partyâs candidates, electoral district associations, officers, agents, employees, volunteers and representatives, must comply with the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information.

For greater certainty, a person or entity referred to in subsection (1) that fails to comply with the policy referred to in that subsection contravenes that subsection and commits a violation referred to in section 508.1.
The policy for the protection of personal information of a registered party or of an eligible party must be publicly available in both official languages, be written in plain language and

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: designate a privacy officer who is responsible for overseeing the partyâs compliance with the policy;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: include the name and contact information of the privacy officer;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: state the types of personal information that the party collects, retains, uses, discloses and disposes of;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: explain, using illustrative examples, how the party collects, retains, uses, discloses and disposes of personal information, such as by indicating whether it does so in the context of online activities or through the use of cookies;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: describe the training related to the protection of personal information that is offered to the partyâs employees and volunteers who may have access to the personal information that is under its control;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: require the party to protect the personal information that is under its control through physical, organizational and technological security safeguards with a level of protection proportionate to the sensitivity of the personal information;

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: require the party to take appropriate steps in the case of the loss of, unauthorized access to or unauthorized disclosure of personal information that is under its control as a result of a breach of its security safeguards, including by, as soon as feasible, informing the individual whose personal information has been lost, accessed or disclosed if it is reasonable in the circumstances to believe the breach creates a real risk of significant harm to the individual;

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: require the party to ensure, by contract or otherwise, that any person or entity to which it transfers personal information provides a level of protection of the personal information equivalent to that which the party is required to provide under the policy;

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: require the privacy officer or their delegate to attend at least one meeting per calendar year relating to the protection of personal information held by the Chief Electoral Officer; and

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: prohibit the party, as well as any person or entity acting on the partyâs behalf, including the partyâs candidates, electoral district associations, officers, agents, employees, volunteers and representatives, from

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: providing false or misleading information to individuals about the purposes for which the party collects personal information,

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: selling personal information under the partyâs control, or

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: disclosing personal information under the partyâs control to the public for the purpose of causing harm.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Real risk of significant harm â factors
Subsection.Text: For the purposes of paragraph (1)(g), the factors that are relevant to determining whether a breach of security safeguards creates a real risk of significant harm to an individual include

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the sensitivity of the personal information involved in the breach; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the probability that the personal information has been, is being or will be misused.

Subsection.Text: For the purposes of this section, significant harm includes bodily harm, humiliation, damage to reputation or relationships, loss of employment, business or professional opportunities, financial loss, identity theft, negative effects on the credit record and damage to or loss of property.

Section.MarginalNote: Meetings relating to protecting personal information
Section.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer shall hold at least one meeting each calendar year relating to the protection of personal information by registered parties and eligible parties.
The heading before section 445 and sections 445 and 446 of the Act are repealed.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 72:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the heading and sections 445 and 446:

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer shall determine, for each quarter of a calendar year, an allowance payable to a registered party whose candidates for the most recent general election preceding that quarter received at that election at least

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: 2% of the number of valid votes cast; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: 5% of the number of valid votes cast in the electoral districts in which the registered party endorsed a candidate.

Subsection.Text: An allowance fund for a quarter is the product of the number of valid votes cast in the election referred to in subsection (1) multiplied by the applicable following number:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: $0.255, for the quarter that begins on April 1, 2013 and the three following quarters; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: $0.1275, for the quarter that begins on April 1, 2014 and the three following quarters.

Subsection.Text: Each such registered partyâs allowance for a quarter is that part of the allowance fund for that quarter that corresponds to its percentage of valid votes cast in the election referred to in subsection (1).
A merged party is entitled to the aggregate of the allowances to which the merging parties of which it is composed would have been entitled had they not merged.

As soon as feasible after the end of each quarter, the Chief Electoral Officer shall provide the Receiver General with a certificate that sets out the amount of the allowance payable to a registered party for that quarter.

If a registered party has not provided all of the documents that it is required to provide under sections 432, 433 and 437, the Chief Electoral Officer shall postpone providing the certificate for any quarter until the party has provided those documents.

On receipt of the certificate, the Receiver General shall pay the amount set out in it to the registered party out of the Consolidated Revenue Fund. The payment may also be made in whole or in part to any provincial division of the party, as authorized in writing by the partyâs leader.

In this Act, provincial division means a division of a registered party for a province or territory in relation to which the partyâs leader has provided the following to the Chief Electoral Officer:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the name of the division and of the province or territory;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the name of the party;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the address of the office where records of that division are maintained and to which communications may be addressed;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the names and addresses of the chief executive officer and other officers of the division;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the name and address of any registered agent appointed by the division; and

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: a declaration signed by the partyâs leader certifying that the division is a division of the party.

ContinuedSectionSubsection.Text: This Act applies to information provided under this subsection as if it were information referred to in paragraphs 385(2)(a) to (h).

Subsection.Text: Within 15 days after a change in the information referred to in subsection (4), the chief executive officer of the provincial division shall report the change in writing to the chief agent of the registered party.

Section.Text: Section 475.4 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: If section 372.2 applies to the registered association, the financial transactions return shall include a list of the measures that the financial agent of the registered association has taken in accordance with that section.

Section.Text: Section 476.75 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: If section 372.2 applies to the nomination contestant, the nomination campaign return shall include a list of the measures that the financial agent of the nomination contestant has taken in accordance with that section.

Section.Text: Section 477.59 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: If section 372.2 applies to the candidate, the electoral campaign return shall include a list of the measures that the official agent of the candidate has taken in accordance with that section.

Section.Text: Subsection 477.94(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Subsection (2) does not prohibit the Commissioner from inspecting the statements referred to in that subsection, and any of those statements may be provided to the Director of Public Prosecutions and produced by the Director for the purpose of a prosecution for an offence under this Act or for a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act.

Subsection (2) does not prohibit the Commissioner from inspecting the statements referred to in that subsection, and any of those statements may be provided to the Director of Public Prosecutions and produced by the Director for the purpose of a prosecution for an offence under this Act.

Section.Text: Section 478.8 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: If section 372.2 applies to the leadership contestant, the leadership campaign return shall include a list of the measures that the financial agent of the leadership contestant has taken in accordance with that section.

Section.Text: Section 480.1 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

Subsection.Text: Subsection (1) applies regardless of the medium of the representation or the manner or place in which the representation is made.
Subsections 481(1) and (2) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Every person or entity is guilty of an offence that distributes, transmits or publishes any material that purports to be made, distributed, transmitted or published by or under the authority of the Chief Electoral Officer, or a returning officer, political party, nomination contestant, candidate, potential candidate, prospective candidate or leadership contestant, if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person or entity was not authorized by the Chief Electoral Officer or that returning officer, political party, nomination contestant, candidate, potential candidate, prospective candidate or leadership contestant to distribute, transmit or publish it; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the person or entity distributes, transmits or publishes it with the intent of misleading the public into believing that it was made, distributed, transmitted or published by or under the authority of the Chief Electoral Officer or that returning officer, political party, nomination contestant, candidate, potential candidate, prospective candidate or leadership contestant.

Subsection.Text: In determining whether a person or entity has committed an offence under subsection (1), the court may consider whether the material included the use of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a name, logo, social media account identifier, username or domain name that is distinctive and commonly associated with the Chief Electoral Officer, a returning officer, or the political party, nomination contestant, candidate, potential candidate, prospective candidate or leadership contestant, as the case may be; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the name, voice, image or signature of the Chief Electoral Officer, a returning officer, or the nomination contestant, candidate, potential candidate, prospective candidate or leadership contestant or of a public figure who is associated with the political party.

Subsection.Text: Every person or entity is guilty of an offence that, during an election period, distributes, transmits or publishes any material, regardless of its form, that purports to be made, distributed, transmitted or published by or under the authority of the Chief Electoral Officer, or a returning officer, political party, candidate or prospective candidate if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person or entity was not authorized by the Chief Electoral Officer or that returning officer, political party, candidate or prospective candidate to distribute, transmit or publish it; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the person or entity distributes, transmits or publishes it with the intent of misleading the public into believing that it was made, distributed, transmitted or published by or under the authority of the Chief Electoral Officer, or that returning officer, political party, candidate or prospective candidate.

Subsection.Text: In determining whether a person or entity has committed an offence under subsection (1) the court may consider whether the material included the use of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a name, logo, social media account identifier, username or domain name that is distinctive and commonly associated with the Chief Electoral Officer, a returning officer, or the political party, candidate or prospective candidate, as the case may be; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the name, voice, image or signature of the Chief Electoral Officer, a returning officer, or the candidate or prospective candidate or of a public figure who is associated with the political party.

Subsection.Text: Section 481 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (3):
Subsection (1) applies regardless of the medium of the material or the manner or place in which the material is made, distributed, transmitted or published.

Section.Text: The portion of subsection 482(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Every person or entity is guilty of an offence that, fraudulently and with the intention of affecting the results or disrupting the conduct of an election,
Every person or entity is guilty of an offence that, fraudulently, and with the intention of affecting the results of an election,

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 482:

Subsection.Text: Every person or entity is guilty of an offence that makes or publishes, with the intention of affecting the results or disrupting the conduct of an election, a statement that they know is false or misleading in respect of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: who may vote in an election, including who is qualified as an elector or may register as an elector;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the process by which an individual may register to vote;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: where, when or the manner in which an individual may vote in an election, including at an advance poll or by special ballot;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: whom an individual may vote for at an election;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the process by which an individual becomes a candidate;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the process by which votes at an election are counted or the results are validated; or

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: the preliminary, validated or official results of an election.

Subsection.Text: Subsection (1) applies regardless of the place where the election is held or the place where the false statement is made or published and regardless of the manner or medium in which the false statement is made or published.
Subsection 486(3) of the Act is amended by striking out âorâ at the end of paragraph (c) and by adding the following after paragraph (d):

Paragraph.Text: knowingly contravenes section 92.2 (filing nomination paper â false or misleading information).

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 495.21:

Subsection.Text: Section 497.01 of the Act is amended by adding âorâ at the end of paragraph (j) and by repealing paragraph (k).
Subsection 500(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Every person who is guilty of an offence under any of subsections 484(1), 489(1), 491(1), 492(1), 495(1), 495.1(1), 495.2(1), 495.21(1), 495.22(1), 495.3(1), 496(1), 496.1(1), 497(1), 497.1(1), 497.2(1), 497.3(1), 497.4(1), 497.5(1), 498(1) and 499(1) is liable on summary conviction to a fine of not more than $2,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than three months, or to both.

Every person who is guilty of an offence under any of subsections 484(1), 489(1), 491(1), 492(1), 495(1), 495.1(1), 495.2(1), 495.21(1), 495.3(1), 496(1), 496.1(1), 497(1), 497.1(1), 497.2(1), 497.3(1), 497.4(1), 497.5(1), 498(1) and 499(1) is liable on summary conviction to a fine of not more than $2,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than three months, or to both.
The portion of subsection 500(5) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Punishment â offences requiring intent (dual procedure)

Section.Text: Section 508 of the Act is replaced by the following:

In a prosecution for an offence under this Act or for a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act, the written statement of the returning officer is, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, sufficient evidence of the holding of the election and of any person named in the certificate having been a candidate.

In a prosecution for an offence under this Act, the written statement of the returning officer is, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, sufficient evidence of the holding of the election and of any person named in the certificate having been a candidate.
Section 508.1 of the Act is replaced by the following:
Every person or entity commits a violation and is liable to an administrative monetary penalty in an amount established in accordance with the provisions of this Act if the person or entity

Paragraph.Text: contravenes section 43.1, any of paragraphs 56(a) to (d), section 81, 81.1, 92.1 or 92.2, subsection 136(4), section 166, 281.3, 281.4, 281.5 or 281.8 or a provision of any of Parts 16, 17 and 18;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: attempts to commit, or conspires with any person or entity to commit, a contravention of a provision referred to in paragraph (a);

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: counsels any person or entity to commit such a contravention, in the case where the contravention is not committed;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: is an accessory after the fact to the commission of such a contravention; or

fails to comply with a requirement of the Chief Electoral Officer under any of Parts 16, 17 and 18, with a provision of a compliance agreement or with a provision of an undertaking that has been accepted by the Commissioner.

Section.Text: Every person or entity that contravenes section 281.3, 281.4 or 281.5 or a provision of any of Parts 16, 17 and 18 â or that fails to comply with a requirement of the Chief Electoral Officer under any of those Parts, with a provision of a compliance agreement or with a provision of an undertaking that has been accepted by the Commissioner â commits a violation and is liable to an administrative monetary penalty in an amount established in accordance with the provisions of this Act.
Section 508.3 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: How act or omission may be proceeded with
Section.Text: If an act or omission may be proceeded with as a violation or as an offence, proceeding in one manner precludes proceeding in the other.
If an act or omission may be proceeded with as a violation or as an offence under this Act, proceeding in one manner precludes proceeding in the other.
Section 508.5 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsections (2) and (3), the maximum administrative monetary penalty for a violation is $1,500, in the case of an individual, and $5,000, in the case of a corporation or an entity.

The maximum administrative monetary penalty for a violation arising from the contravention of section 349.04, 349.05, 363, 367, 372.1 or 372.2 is an amount equal to twice the amount that was contributed â accepted or not returned, not destroyed, or not converted and paid, as the case may be â in contravention of that section, plus

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: $1,500, in the case of an individual; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: $5,000, in the case of a corporation or entity.

Subsection.Text: The maximum administrative monetary penalty for a violation arising from the contravention of subsection 349.95(1) or 358(1) is an amount equal to twice the amount of the contribution that was used in contravention of that subsection, plus $5,000.
Subject to subsection (2), the maximum administrative monetary penalty for a violation is $1,500, in the case of an individual, and $5,000, in the case of a corporation or an entity.

The maximum administrative monetary penalty for a violation arising from the contravention of section 363 or 367 is an amount equal to twice the amount that was contributed in contravention of that section, plus

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: $1,500, in the case of an individual who contravenes section 363 or 367; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: $5,000, in the case of a corporation or an entity that contravenes section 363.

Section.Text: Section 509.22 of the Act is renumbered as subsection 509.22(1) and is amended by adding the following:

Subsection.Text: For greater certainty, the Commissioner may, in the exercise or performance of the Commissionerâs powers, duties and functions under this Part, enter into memoranda of understanding or other arrangements with a department or body that is listed in a schedule to the Financial Administration Act and has technical or specialized knowledge on matters of national security or any other matters related to those powers, duties and functions.

Paragraph.Text: produce to the Commissioner or the authorized representative, within a time and at a place specified in the order, a record â or a copy of a record certified by affidavit to be a true copy â or any other thing specified in the order; or

Subsection.Text: Subsection 510.01(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:

No order may be made under subsection (1) against an individual who is alleged to have committed, or who is about to commit, the contravention â or the conspiracy, attempt, being an accessory after the fact or counselling â to which the application relates.
No order may be made under subsection (1) against an individual who is alleged to have committed, or who is about to commit, the contravention to which the application relates.

Section.Text: Paragraph 510.1(2)(d) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (d)

Section.Text: Section 511 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: If the Commissioner believes on reasonable grounds that an offence under this Act â or that a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act â has been committed, the Commissioner may institute a prosecution or cause one to be instituted.
The prosecution is instituted by the laying of an information in writing and under oath before a justice, as defined in section 2 of the Criminal Code.
If the Commissioner believes on reasonable grounds that an offence under this Act has been committed, the Commissioner may institute a prosecution or cause one to be instituted.
A prosecution for an offence under this Act is instituted by the laying of an information in writing and under oath before a justice, as defined in section 2 of the Criminal Code.

Section.Text: Subsection 512(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: No prosecution for an offence under this Act â or for a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act â may be instituted by a person, other than the Commissioner or a person acting under the Commissionerâs direction, without the prior written consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions provided after consultation with the Commissioner.

No prosecution for an offence under this Act may be instituted by a person, other than the Commissioner or a person acting under his or her direction, without the prior written consent of the Director of Public Prosecutions provided after consultation with the Commissioner.
Subsection 514(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Proceedings in respect of an offence under a provision set out in subsection 500(1) â or in respect of a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, such an offence â may be commenced at any time within, but not later than, six years after the day on which the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.

Proceedings in respect of an offence under a provision set out in subsection 500(1) may be commenced at any time within, but not later than, six years after the day on which the subject-matter of the proceedings arose.
Subsection 514(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Proceedings in respect of an offence under a provision set out in any of subsections 500(2) to (5) â or in respect of a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, such an offence â may be commenced at any time.
Proceedings in respect of an offence under a provision set out in any of subsections 500(2) to (5) may be commenced at any time.
Subsection 516(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

If the Commissioner has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that is contrary to this Act â or an act or omission that constitutes a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, a contravention of this Act â the Commissioner may, during an election period, after taking into account the nature and seriousness of the act or omission, the need to ensure fairness of the electoral process and the public interest, apply to a competent court described in subsection 525(1) for an injunction described in subsection (2).

If the Commissioner has reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that is contrary to this Act, the Commissioner may, during an election period, after taking into account the nature and seriousness of the act or omission, the need to ensure fairness of the electoral process and the public interest, apply to a competent court described in subsection 525(1) for an injunction described in subsection (2).
The portion of subsection 516(2) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

If the court, on application by the Commissioner under subsection (1), is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that is contrary to this Act â or an act or omission that constitutes a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, a contravention of this Act â and that the nature and seriousness of the act or omission, the need to ensure fairness of the electoral process and the public interest justify the issuing of an injunction, the court may issue an injunction ordering any person named in the application to do one or both of the following:

If the court, on application by the Commissioner under subsection (1), is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that a person has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that is contrary to this Act, and that the nature and seriousness of the act or omission, the need to ensure fairness of the electoral process and the public interest justify the issuing of an injunction, the court may issue an injunction ordering any person named in the application to do one or both of the following:
Subsection 517(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Power to enter into compliance agreement

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (7), the Commissioner may enter into a compliance agreement, aimed at ensuring compliance with this Act, with a person or entity (in this section and sections 518 to 521 called the âcontracting partyâ) if the Commissioner believes on reasonable grounds that the person or entity has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that could constitute an offence under this Act â or that could constitute a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act.

Subject to subsection (7), if the Commissioner believes on reasonable grounds that a person or entity has committed, is about to commit or is likely to commit an act or omission that could constitute an offence under this Act, the Commissioner may enter into a compliance agreement, aimed at ensuring compliance with this Act, with the person or entity (in this section and sections 518 to 521 called the âcontracting partyâ).
Subsection 517(4) of the Act is replaced by the following:
A compliance agreement may include a statement by the contracting party in which it admits responsibility for the act or omission in question.
A compliance agreement may include a statement by the contracting party in which it admits responsibility for the act or omission that constitutes the offence.

Section.Text: Section 521.24 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Every rule and principle of the common law that renders any circumstance a justification or excuse in relation to a charge for any offence applies in respect of a violation to the extent that it is not inconsistent with this Act.

Every rule and principle of the common law that renders any circumstance a justification or excuse in relation to a charge for any offence under this Act applies in respect of a violation to the extent that it is not inconsistent with this Act.
Section 521.27 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A person or entity is a party to a violation committed by another person or entity and is liable for the violation if it

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: does or omits to do anything for the purpose of aiding that other person or entity to commit the violation;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: abets that other person or entity in committing the violation; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: counsels that other person or entity to commit the violation.

Subsection.Text: If an entity commits a violation, any of the entityâs directors, officers or agents or mandataries who directed, authorized, assented to, acquiesced in or participated in the commission of the violation is a party to and liable for the violation.
Subsections (1) and (2) apply whether or not the person or entity that actually committed the violation is proceeded against under this Act.

Section.Text: If an entity commits a violation, any of the entityâs directors, officers or agents or mandataries who directed, authorized, assented to, acquiesced in or participated in the commission of the violation is a party to and liable for the violation whether or not the entity that actually committed the violation is proceeded against under this Act.
Subsection 538(5) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A returning officer may, with the approval of the Chief Electoral Officer, constitute polling divisions that consist of one or more institutions, or part of an institution, where seniors or persons with a disability reside.
A returning officer may, with the approval of the Chief Electoral Officer, constitute polling divisions that consist of two or more institutions where seniors or persons with a disability reside.

Section.Text: Subsection 540(4.1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer may also disclose any of the documents referred to in subsection (3) to the Commissioner for the purposes of the exercise or performance of the Commissionerâs powers, duties and functions under this Act and the Commissioner may, in turn, disclose any of those documents to the Director of Public Prosecutions, who may produce them for the purpose of a prosecution â or possible prosecution â by the Director for an offence under this Act or for a conspiracy or attempt to commit, or being an accessory after the fact or counselling in relation to, an offence under this Act.

The Chief Electoral Officer may also disclose any of the documents referred to in subsection (3) to the Commissioner for the purposes of the exercise or performance of the Commissionerâs powers, duties and functions under this Act and the Commissioner may, in turn, disclose any of those documents to the Director of Public Prosecutions, who may produce them for the purpose of a prosecution â or possible prosecution â by the Director for an offence under this Act.
The portion of subsection 549.1(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

For the purposes of subsections 143(3) and (3.2), sections 144 and 147 and paragraphs 161(1)(b), 169(2)(b) and 235(c), the solemn declaration by which an elector proves his or her identity and residence, proves his or her residence only, proves that he or she is qualified as an elector or proves that he or she has not previously voted at the election shall be in the prescribed form, which shall include the statements that

For the purposes of subsections 143(3) and (3.2), sections 144 and 147 and paragraphs 161(1)(b) and 169(2)(b), the solemn declaration by which an elector proves his or her identity and residence, proves his or her residence only, proves that he or she is qualified as an elector or proves that he or she has not previously voted at the election shall be in the prescribed form, which shall include the statements that

Paragraph.Text: Â

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the elector has not previously voted in the election and is not an elector to whom section 235 applies.

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 549.1(1)(d) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the elector has not previously voted in the election and will not vote more than once in the election, including by using a special ballot.

Heading.TitleText: Reports to Speaker of the House of Commons

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer must make a report to the Speaker of the House of Commons on the measures that need to be taken to implement a three-day polling period for general elections for which the writs are issued after January 1, 2029, including any necessary amendments to the Canada Elections Act.
In the report, the Chief Electoral Officer must set out the Chief Electoral Officerâs opinion respecting

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the costs of, the necessary technology for and any challenges related to the implementation of a three-day polling period, as well as solutions to those challenges;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the date by which each measure needs to be taken if the Chief Electoral Officer is of the opinion that it is feasible for the measures to be taken for the first general election for which the writs are issued after January 1, 2029; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the date by which it would be feasible to take each measure if the Chief Electoral Officer is of the opinion that it is not feasible for the measures to be taken for the first general election for which the writs are issued after January 1, 2029.

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer must make the report as soon as feasible after the first general election that is held after the day on which this section comes into force.

Section.MarginalNote: Report â vote at any place in polling station

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer must make a report to the Speaker of the House of Commons on the measures that need to be taken to provide each elector with a ballot at any place in their polling station where ballots are provided to electors for the first general election that is held on or after the next fixed election date and that is held after the making of the report.
The Chief Electoral Officer must make the report as soon as feasible, and in any event no later than the 120th day before the next fixed election date.

Section.MarginalNote: Report â vote at any polling station

Subsection.Text: The Chief Electoral Officer must, no later than January 1, 2027, make a report to the Speaker of the House of Commons on the feasibility of enabling electors to vote at any polling station in their electoral district. In the report, the Chief Electoral Officer must set out the Chief Electoral Officerâs opinion respecting

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the measures that need to be taken to enable electors to vote at any polling station in their electoral district, including any necessary amendments to the Canada Elections Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the date by which each measure needs to be taken if the Chief Electoral Officer is of the opinion that it is feasible to enable electors to vote at any polling station in the electoral district for the first general election for which the writs are issued after January 1, 2029; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the date by which it would be feasible to take each measure if the Chief Electoral Officer is of the opinion that it is not feasible to enable electors to vote at any polling station in the electoral district for the first general election for which the writs are issued after January 1, 2029.

Subsection.Text: Before making the report, the Chief Electoral Officer must consult with the Advisory Committee of Political Parties, established by subsection 21.1(1) of the Canada Elections Act.

The Chief Electoral Officer must make a report to the Speaker of the House of Commons setting out a proposed process to determine whether a registered party or an eligible party has as one of its fundamental purposes the promotion of hatred against an identifiable group of persons, as well as setting out the proposed consequences of such a determination.

Before making the report, the Chief Electoral Officer must consult with the Commissioner of Canada Elections and the Advisory Committee of Political Parties established by subsection 21.1(1) of the Canada Elections Act.
The Chief Electoral Officer must make the report as soon as feasible, and in any event no later than the 120th day before the next fixed election date.

Section.Text: The Speaker of the House of Commons must submit each report received under sections 110 to 113 to the House of Commons without delay.

Section.MarginalNote: Alternative voting process or voting technology
Section.Text: Subject to subsections 18.1(5) and (6) of the Canada Elections Act, any alternative voting process or voting technology approved for use under subsection 18.1(4) of that Act during the 44th Parliament may be used in one or more elections held within six years after the day on which the approval is granted.

Section.MarginalNote: Policy for the protection of personal information â parties already registered, etc.

Subsection.Text: Within three months after the day on which this section comes into force, the leader of a political party must provide the Chief Electoral Officer with the partyâs policy for the protection of personal information referred to in subsection 444.4(1) of the Canada Elections Act if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: before the day on which this section comes into force, the leader of the party has applied under section 385 of that Act for the party to become a registered party but, as of that day, the Chief Electoral Officer has not yet informed the leader under subsection 389(1) of that Act whether the party is eligible under section 387 of that Act for registration; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which this section comes into force, the party is

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: an eligible party, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a registered party.

Subsection.Text: If the leader of the political party does not comply with subsection (1), then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in paragraph (1)(a), the party is not eligible under section 387 of the Canada Elections Act for registration;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in subparagraph (1)(b)(i), the party is not permitted to become a registered party under section 390 of that Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a party referred to in subparagraph (1)(b)(ii), the Chief Electoral Officer shall implement the procedure for non-voluntary deregistration set out in sections 415, 416 and 418 of that Act.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Deemed inclusion in application for registration
Subsection.Text: If the leader of a political party provides the Chief Electoral Officer with the policy referred to in subsection (1) in compliance with that subsection, or in compliance with a notice referred to in subsection 415(1) of the Canada Elections Act or a notice amended under subsection 415(2) of that Act, then the policy is deemed, as of the day on which it is provided, to be included in the application for registration referred to in subsection 385(2) of that Act in respect of the party.

Section.Text: Paragraph 387(d) of the Canada Elections Act, does not apply in respect of applications made under subsection 385(1) of that Act before the day on which that paragraph comes into force.

Subsection.Text: The words and expressions used in sections 110 to 117 have the same meaning as in the Canada Elections Act.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Definition of next fixed election date
Subsection.Text: In section 111, subsection 113(3) and section 120, next fixed election date means the day set in accordance with subsection 56.1(2) of the Canada Elections Act for the holding of the first general election after the day on which this Act receives royal assent.

Section.Text: Despite subsection 554(1) of the Canada Elections Act, the amendments to that Act made by sections 62, 65 to 69 and 71 and subsection 89(1) of this Act apply to an election for which the writ is issued within six months after the day on which this Act receives royal assent.
Section 6 comes into force on the day after the next fixed election date.
